coredata.dc:title: Ultra-stable and long-lived multi-color room temperature phosphorescent carbon dot and silica composites for data encryption and anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Xin
Liu, Zixuan
Tian, Zhen
Sun, Wenquan
Wang, Hui
Yuan, Xi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dot (CD)-based room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have garnered fundamental attention due to their unique and irreplaceable advantages. However, developing CD-based materials with stable multi-color RTP emission is also desired. Herein, we present a rational design of carbon dots and SiO2 composite (CD@ Silica) fabrication, which demonstrates excellent stability in its RTP, showcases multi-color emission, and experiences thermal enhancement. Multi-color afterglow emission is achieved in CD/fluorescence dyes@ Silica composite systems due to the high efficiency of the Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) processes between CDs and fluorescence dyes. The covalent interactions between CDs and silica shells protect the triplet state, and the structure significantly promotes the multi-color RTP emission realized in some challenging environments (such as in acid or alkaline aqueous solution). Utilizing the stable phosphorescence emission, we successfully implement the concepts of multi-level information encryption and fingerprint anti-counterfeiting through CD@ Silica composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multi-color room temperature phosphorescent
Stability
Anti-counterfeiting
Covalent bonds

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of blue fluorescence emissive CDs (b-CDs) and CD@ silica composite
Synthesis of CD@ silica composites with multi-color phosphorescence
Preparation of Phos. Ink-1 and Phos. Ink-2
The morphological characterization of CD@ silica composites
The photophysical characterization of CD@ silica composites
The stability and mechanism of multi-color phosphorescence

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Development of Science and Technology of Jilin Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology of Jilin Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Development of Science and Technology of Jilin Province ( 20210101161JC , YDZJ202301ZYTS254 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of b-CDs. a) TEM image of b-CDs, insets show HRTEM images of b-CDs. b) Excitation-emission maps of an aqueous solution of b-CDs. c) FTIR spectrum of b-CDs. d) Full survey XPS spectra of b-CDs.

Morphological characterization of CD@ Silica composites. a) TEM image, b) FTIR spectrum, and c) full survey XPS spectra of CD@ Silica composite. d-f) High-resolution XPS spectra and fitting results of C1s, O1s, and Si2p spectra of the composite, respectively.

a) Normalized absorption spectra of the aqueous solution of b-CDs and CD@ Silica composite powders. b) Excitation-emission maps, c) temperature-dependent afterglow emission spectra, d) prompt and delayed spectra under 77 K, e) afterglow emission spectra after annealing at different temperatures, and f) phosphorescence decay curves of CD@ Silica composite after annealing at different temperatures.

a) Overlapping of the phosphorescence spectra of CD@ Silica and UV–vis absorption spectra of Rh6G and RhB. b) Absorption spectra of CD@ Silica and CD/fluorescence dyes@ Silica composites. c) Bright field, fluorescence, and phosphorescence images of composite powders. Phosphorescence emission spectra of d) CD/Rh6G@Silica and e) CD/RhB@ Silica composites. f) Phosphorescence decay curves of CD@ Silica, CD/fluorescence dyes@ Silica composites with different concentrations of Rh6G and RhB, monitored at an emission of 510 nm, 560 nm, and 610 nm (under 360 nm excitation), respectively.

a) Schematic illustration for preparing the CD/fluorescence dyes@ silica composite and proposed mechanism of the photophysical processes in these composites. b) Changes of afterglow intensity of multi-color CDs@ Silica composites at different PH conditions. c) Changes of afterglow intensity of CDs@ Silica composites under UV irradiation for up to 5 days.

Application of CD@ Silica composites. a) Schematic presentation of multiple encrypted patterns using CD@ Silica composites (Phos.ink-1), commercial fluorescent dye (Fluo. dye), and CD@ PVA phosphorescent composites (Phos.ink-2) via UV on/off before and after water spray. b) Bright field, fluorescence, and phosphorescence photographs of a fingerprint made of CD@ Silica powders.
Schematic illustration for the formation procedure of blue fluorescent CDs and CD@ Silica RTP composites.

head.ce:title: Ultra-stable and long-lived multi-color room temperature phosphorescent carbon dot and silica composites for data encryption and anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University, Changchun 130103, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Physics and Chemistry of the Ministry of Education, Jilin Normal University, Changchun 130103, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Aviation University of Air Force, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Aviation University of Air Force, Changchun 130012, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Key Laboratory of Solid-State Laser Technology and Application, School of Physics, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Key Laboratory of Solid-State Laser Technology and Application
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jilin Key Laboratory of Solid-State Laser Technology and Application, School of Physics, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dot (CD)-based room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have garnered fundamental attention due to their unique and irreplaceable advantages. However, developing CD-based materials with stable multi-color RTP emission is also desired. Herein, we present a rational design of carbon dots and SiO2 composite (CD@ Silica) fabrication, which demonstrates excellent stability in its RTP, showcases multi-color emission, and experiences thermal enhancement. Multi-color afterglow emission is achieved in CD/fluorescence dyes@ Silica composite systems due to the high efficiency of the Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) processes between CDs and fluorescence dyes. The covalent interactions between CDs and silica shells protect the triplet state, and the structure significantly promotes the multi-color RTP emission realized in some challenging environments (such as in acid or alkaline aqueous solution). Utilizing the stable phosphorescence emission, we successfully implement the concepts of multi-level information encryption and fingerprint anti-counterfeiting through CD@ Silica composites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Long-lived room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have promising practical applications owing to their unique advantages, such as a long-lived luminescence lifetime, a high signal-to-noise ratio, and visibility to the naked eye [1–7]. As an emerging class of nano-optical carbon-based materials, carbon dots (CDs) and their composites with RTP emission sparked interest in data security, sensing, bio-imaging, etc [8–16]. Compared to traditional commercial afterglow materials, mainly composed of inorganic coordination compounds and organic compounds containing rare-earth elements, CDs afterglow materials exhibit lower costs, reduced toxicity, and simplified preparation processes. Several strategies have been effectively implemented to generate or enhance the phosphorescence in CDs-based materials. For instance, the inclusion of heteroatom (such as N, P, F, B, etc.) into CDs to facilitate the spin-orbit coupling [17–20], or embedding CDs within some matrix materials to develop rigid composite structures [21–24], which could protect the triplet state and promote the intersystem crossing (ISC) process.

Nonetheless, most CDs-based composite materials undergo rapid quenching in humid environments due to the non-radiative deactivation of triplet excitons by dissolved oxygen in water [25]. In addition, while the RTP color produced by most CD composites is typically located in the green spectrum, phosphorescent emission at longer wavelengths ranging from yellow to red light is quite rare [26–28]. Silica is a common matrix for fabricating luminescent composites, and numerous research groups have recently developed anti-water multi-color RTP emissions by embedding CDs within a silica matrix [29–33]. Along these lines, Shan's group embedded microwave-synthesized CDs into silica and prepared CDs-silica composite aqueous solution with green phosphorescence emission (quantum yield (QY): 11.6 %). Furthermore, they successfully developed the red color phosphorescence emission by establishing the Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) process [34]. Similarly, Liu and colleagues realized multi-color phosphorescence (green to red) in water through cascade FRET processes in amorphous CDs-silica systems [35]. However, it is still a challenge to maintain the multi-color phosphorescence emission in some complex environments (e.g., acid, alkaline, ultrasonic, or UV light irradiation). Developing stable phosphorescent emission in various acidic and alkaline environments is crucial for some applications, such as fingerprint anti-counterfeiting.

In this work, we initially synthesized novel P, N co-doped CDs exhibiting blue fluorescence and a weaker green RTP emission via a hydrothermal method. A high-efficiency green anti-water RTP was developed by incorporating CDs into a silica matrix. The composite exhibited a remarkable QY of 14.2 %. Further enhancement of the RTP intensity was achieved by annealing the composites at an optimized temperature, resulting in an average lifetime of 1064 ms. Multi-color phosphorescent emission ranging from green to red light in CD/fluorescence dyes@ Silica composite system was constructed through FRET processes between CDs and fluorescence dyes. Notably, the multi-color phosphorescence stability of these composites was systematically investigated. These composites exhibit stable phosphorescent emission in acid, alkaline, and UV light irradiation environments. Thus, utilizing the unique characteristics of the CD@ Silica composites, new concepts for multi-level information encryption and fingerprint anti-counterfeiting were developed.

Ethylenediamine (EDA), Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA), Rhodamine B (RhB), and Rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) were purchased from Macklin. Phosphoric acid (PA) and ammonium hydroxide were purchased from Fuyu, and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was purchased from Aladdin. Deionized water used in the experiments was purified by a purification system. All chemicals were directly used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of blue fluorescence emissive CDs (b-CDs) and CD@ silica composite
ce:section.ce:para: The b-CDs and CD@ Silica composite preparation process are shown in Scheme 1 b-CDs were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method with ethylenediamine (EDA) and phosphoric acid (PA) as precursors. First, 1 mL of EDA solution and 2 mL of phosphoric acid were dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water, heated at the temperature of 180 °C for 3 h under hydrothermal conditions in a reaction autoclave and cooled to room temperature, and the yellow solution was acquired. Then, the yellow solution was centrifuged at 3000 r min−1 for 5 min and filtered through 0.22 μm membrane to remove the sediment, dialyzed in a dialysis bag (500 Da) against water for 48 h. Finally, the solution was collected, freeze-dried into a yellow product, and ground into powder.

4 mg b-CDs powders, 1 mL of TEOS solution, and 1 mL of ammonia were dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water, respectively. We named the mixed solution as “seed solution-1”. After reacting at room temperature for 5 h, the mixed solution acquire a blue fluorescence and green phosphorescence emission, we named the solution as “seed solution-2”. Then seed solution-2 was freeze-dried into a white solid product, and after grounding we acquire the CD@ Silica composite powders with green phosphorescence. It is note worth that the afterglow emission intensity could be enhanced after the powders annealed at a higher temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CD@ silica composites with multi-color phosphorescence
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mL Rhodamine-6G (Rh6G) or Rhodamine B (RhB) solutions with different concentrations (0.5 mg/mL, 1 mg/mL, 2 mg/L, and 4 mg/mL) were added in a 20 mL seed solution-1. The mixed solution reacted at room temperature for 5 h, and the CD@ Silica solution with different phosphorescence emissions was acquired. Then the solution was collected and freeze-dried into a solid product. After grounding and annealing, the CD@ silica composites with multi-color phosphorescence were prepared.
For the powders measured in fluorescence/phosphorescence measurement, the mass ratio of b-CDs and dyes in composite powders are 1:0.125, 1: 0.25, 1:0.5, and 1:1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Phos. Ink-1 and Phos. Ink-2
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 mL seed solution-2 was mixed with 1.5 mL PVA aqueous solution, and 3 mg b-CDs were mixed with 6 mL PVA aqueous solution. After ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, we acquired Phos. ink-1 and Phos. ink-2, respectively. Notably, the efficient phosphorescent emission from the information written in Phos. ink-2 could be acquired after annealing at a high temperature (180 °C).

TEM images were obtained on a JEM-2100 plus transmission electron microscope, and SEM images were obtained on a JSM 5600LV scanning electron microscope. XPS analysis was performed on a Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab spectrometer using Mg as the excitation source, and the FTIR spectra were obtained with a Perkin–Elmer spectrometer, and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data were collected on a Siemens D5005 diffractometer. TGA curves were measured on an American TA Q500 analyzer under a nitrogen atmosphere. UV–visible absorption spectra were collected from a Shimadzu UV-3101PC spectrophotometer. Lifetimes and fluorescence/phosphorescence spectra were carried out using a spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3) with time-correlated single-photon counting lifetime spectroscopy and phosphorescent systems. For the phosphorescence spectra, the time between flashes is 500 ms, the flash count is 40, the initial delay is 5 ms, and the sample window is 200 ms. The photoluminescence quantum yield was measured by Otsuka QE-2000 with an integrating sphere.

For the phosphorescence spectra, the time between flashes is 500 ms, flash count is 40, initial delay is 5 ms, and sample window is 200 ms. Time-resolved phosphorescence decay curves were collected under 360 nm excitation. Average luminescence lifetimes were calculated using the following equation:
τ avg
=
∑
a i
τ i 2
∑
a i
τ i
Time-resolved luminescence decay curves were fitted with tri-exponential functions.

The aqueous solution of b-CDs displayed a blue fluorescence emission under 365 nm UV light, while only a weaker green afterglow signal was observed in the b-CDs powders. The morphologies of the b-CDs were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), revealing diameters ranging from 2 to 4 nm (Fig. S1), as depicted in Fig. 1 a. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image was also shown in the inset of Fig. 1a and revealed that the b-CDs possessed clear lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the (100) facet of graphite [36]. The fluorescence excitation-emission map of the aqueous solution of b-CDs (Fig. 1b) showed a blue emission with its most intense peak at 420 nm when excited at 360 nm. According to the XRD patterns (Fig. S2) of the b-CDs, a signal appeared at 25°, indicative of the typical peak associated with lattice spacing of graphite, as previously reported [37]. Furthermore, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed to confirm the surface structure of these blue-emitting CDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, the absorption peaks centered at 3440 cm−1 and 2988 cm−1 belonged to O–H and –CH2 stretching vibrations, respectively. Meanwhile, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis revealed the chemical composition of b-CDs, as presented in Fig. 1d. The atomic ratios of C, N, O, and P elements were found to be 25.47 %, 13.39 %, 47.63 %, and 13.51 %, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of b-CDs are shown in Fig. S3, where the C1s spectrum showed the existence of C–C (284.8 eV) and C–N (286.3 eV) bonds. In addition, the N1s spectrum displayed the existence of C–NC (398.6 eV) and N–H (400.2 eV) bonds. In the O1s spectrum, three distinct peaks were observed at 530.3 eV, 531.5 eV, and 532.3 eV, attributing to PO, C–O–C/C–OH, and P–O bonds [34], respectively. Besides, the P2s spectrum showcased evidence of P–O (132.8 eV) and P–N (133.7 eV) bonds. Based on these results, we considered that the green RTP observed in b-CDs powders emerged from incorporating the N and P elements, thus facilitating the ISC progress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphological characterization of CD@ silica composites
ce:section.ce:para: Notably, upon incorporating b-CDs into a silica matrix, the afterglow properties of the CD-based composites saw significant enhancement. b-CDs were encapsulated into nano-silica through the hydrolysis reaction of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), and a white precipitate exhibiting green RTP emission in the aqueous solution appeared after 3 h. We obtained CD@ Silica composite powders via freeze drying, exhibiting blue fluorescence and green phosphorescence under UV light (as shown in Fig. S4). To gain a clearer understanding of the morphology of the CD@ Silica composite, both TEM and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were captured. In Fig. 2 a, the TEM image of the composite showed an aggregated spherical morphology with CDs encapsulated by silica, and the particle size of CDs has no obvious change (Fig. S5). The SEM image (Fig. S6) further confirmed this observation, demonstrating that the diameter of these spherical particles ranged from 10 to 40 nm. Moreover, an FTIR spectrum was analyzed to validate the structure of the CD@ Silica composite (Fig. 2b), where the absorption peaks centered at 794 cm−1 and 1090 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of Si–OH and Si–O–Si, respectively. Fig. 2c displays the XPS spectra of the CD@ Silica composite, confirming the presence of C, N, O, P, and Si elements in the composite. The high-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s, shown in Fig. 2d, are subdivided into three distinct peaks located at 284.3 eV, 285 eV, and 286.6 eV, which were assigned to C–Si, C–C/CC, and CO, respectively. Furthermore, the high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 2e) indicated the presence of CO (531.7 eV) and Si–O (532.9 eV) bonds. Meanwhile, the Si2p spectra (Fig. 2f) were deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 102.7 eV, 103.4 eV, and 104.0 eV, belonging to C–Si, Si–O, and SiO2 (gel) [32]. Thus, FTIR and XPS combined results further verified that b-CDs were securely embedded within the silica matrix via covalent bonds in the CD@ Silica composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photophysical characterization of CD@ silica composites
ce:section.ce:para: The CD@ Silica composite powders exhibited a green afterglow emission at room temperature, easily noticeable to the naked eye for more than 10 s after removing the 365 nm UV excitation source. Subsequently, an in-depth exploration of the photophysical properties of the CD@ Silica composites was conducted. The absorption spectra of b-CDs and the composite are shown in Fig. 3 a. Notably, the aqueous solution of b-CDs and composite powders displayed a peak centered at 340 nm, which can be attributed to n-π* transitions in b-CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence excitation-emission spectrum of CD@ Silica composite powders is shown in Fig. 3b. A broader emission center that spans from blue to green was evident when excited at 350–420 nm. Eventually, the temperature-dependent afterglow spectra of the CD@ Silica composite were acquired and are presented in Fig. 3c. The phosphorescence emission was centered around 500–510 nm, and its intensity decreased with the increase in temperature, indicative of its RTP nature, suggesting the suppression of non-radiative transitions (vibrations) at lower temperatures. The delayed and prompt emission spectra of the CD@ Silica composite at 77 K, as shown in Fig. 3d, revealed that the energy gap (△EST) between the triplet and singlet states for the as-prepared composite was determined to be 0.35 eV. It should be noted here that the RTP intensity of the CD@ Silica composite became stronger when the powders were annealed at elevated temperatures. As illustrated in Fig. 3e, these powders were annealed at different temperatures ranging from 300 K to 420 K for 10 min. While the RTP emission center remained relatively stable, the intensity increased and decreased with the increasing temperature. The highest intensity in these powders was achieved at around 390 K, with the phosphorescence quantum yield reaching 14.2 % (Fig. S7). The time-resolved phosphorescence decay curves of the CD@ Silica composite powders, annealed at various temperatures and collected at 510 nm, are shown in Fig. 3f. These decay curves can be accurately fitted using a tri-exponential function, and the powders annealed at 390 K showed the longest average lifetime of 1064 ms.

The FRET process is a reliable method for tuning the photoluminescent emission in CDs-based composite systems, requiring sufficient spectral overlap between donor emission and acceptor absorption [38–40]. Moreover, maintaining a less than 10 nm distance between the donor and acceptor materials is also crucial for efficient energy transfer. Two fluorescence dyes (Rh6G and RhB) were selected as energy acceptors in the CD@ Silica composite to realize multi-color phosphorescent emission. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, the green phosphorescence emission from the CD@ Silica composite, when excited at 360 nm, overlapped well with the absorption bands of Rh6G and RhB. Both the TEM and SEM images demonstrated that the diameter of these composites was within the range of 10–40 nm, thus achieving enough spectral overlap and proximity between the b-CDs (donor materials) and dyes (acceptor materials). The interplay of the factors above resulted in the occurrence of the FRET process within this system, enabling the preparation of CD/fluorescence dyes@ Silica composites showcasing multi-color afterglow emission. The encapsulation of CDs and fluorescence dyes into silica shells prevented aggregation-induced quenching. Notably, CD@ Silica composite powders prepared with different concentrations of fluorescence dyes exhibited similar fluorescence emission peaks at 560 nm and 610 nm (Fig. S8). Furthermore, the absorption spectra of CD@ Silica, CD/Rh6G@ Silica, and CD/RhB@ Silica composites were investigated and are shown in Fig. 4b. All the powder samples displayed an absorption peak between 350 and 360 nm, stemming from the b-CDs.

The CD/fluorescence dyes@ Silica composites exhibited absorption peaks at 527 nm and 553 nm, resembling the absorption characteristics of Rh6G and RhB, respectively. Meanwhile, yellow and red phosphorescent emissions were observed from these composite powders after removing the UV excitation source (Fig. 4c). The multi-color afterglow emission spectra of these composite powders are shown in Fig. 4d and e. Specifically, samples with different mass ratios of CD/Rh6G exhibited a similar yellow phosphorescent emission located around 550–560 nm. Similarly, composites prepared with various concentrations of RhB demonstrated a red afterglow emission around 610–620 nm. In the CD/fluorescence dyes@ Silica composites, the phosphorescence intensity initially increased and then declined with the rise in the concentration of fluorescence dye. This behavior can be attributed to the aggregation-induced quenching effects of fluorescence dyes and the varying energy transfer efficiency within these phosphorescent composites. Another phosphorescence emission peak, centered at 500 nm, was ascribed to the donor materials (b-CDs) involved in the FRET processes, which diminished progressively with a decrease in the mass ratio of CD/fluorescence dyes, highlighting the increasing energy transfer efficiency between b-CDs and fluorescence dyes. The time-resolved phosphorescence decay curves of CD@ Silica (collected at 510 nm), CD/Rh6G@ Silica (collected at 560 nm), and CD/RhB@ Silica (collected at 610 nm) composites under 360 nm excitation are shown in Fig. 4f. Based on the deconvolution fitting of these decay curves, the average phosphorescence lifetimes of CD/Rh6G @Silica (1:0.25) and CD/RhB@ Silica (1:0.125) were estimated to be 488.2 ms and 415.5 ms, respectively.

In addition, the time-resolved phosphorescence decay curves of CD@ Silica, CD/Rh6G@ Silica, and CD/RhB@ Silica with different mass ratios of CDs to dyes at 510 nm (under 360 nm excitation) are depicted in Fig. S9. The lifetimes of CD@ Silica showed a noticeable decline as more dyes were introduced, indicating the occurrence of energy transfer in composites. The energy transfer efficiency (E) can be calculated using the relationship: E = 1- (τDA/τD), where τDA and τD denote the phosphorescence lifetimes of CD/dyes@ Silica and CD@ Silica composites, respectively [41]. For CD/Rh6G@ Silica and CD/RhB@ Silica composites, at a mass ratio of CD to dyes of 1:1, the energy transfer efficiencies were determined to be 55.8 % and 62.7 %, which were higher than when the mass ratio was 1:0.125 (as presented in Fig. S10). The high energy transfer efficiency ensures the multi-color RTP emission in CD/dyes@ Silica composite systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability and mechanism of multi-color phosphorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the detailed mechanisms for the thermally enhanced multi-color RTP emission observed in these CD@ Silica composite system were proposed. Drawing from the results of the absorption spectra, the origin of RTP appeared to stem from the n-π* transitions on the surface of b-CDs. Phosphorescence emission originated from the relaxation of excitation energy through radiative transitions from the triplet state. For the CD@ Silica composite, b-CDs were embedded in the silica matrix through covalent bonds, as verified by the results of FTIR and XPS. This structure prevented aggregation-induced quenching, enhanced the ISC process considerably, and suppressed the non-radiative transitions. As a result, the CD@ Silica composite powders exhibited blue fluorescence and green phosphorescence. However, following the hydrolysis reaction of TEOS and the freeze-drying process, numerous functional groups (such as –OH) remained on the surface of b-CDs, which were not bonded to the silica shell. Upon annealing at an appropriate temperature, a dehydration reaction between these functional groups and the silica shells occurred and resulted in the formation of many new covalent bonds, greatly enhancing the rigidity of the matrix materials. The carbonation reaction between these functional groups can adversely influence the RTP emission under higher annealing temperatures (>390 K), so the strongest RTP emission from the CD@ Silica composite was observed when the composite was annealed at around 390 K. Similar phenomenon also could be observed in CD/fluorescence dyes@ Silica composites (as shown in Fig. S11). In the case of CD/fluorescence dyes@ Silica composites, both b-CDs and fluorescence dyes were encapsulated in the silica matrix through hydrolysis. This ensured that the distance between the donor and acceptor was sufficiently close while maintaining the substantial spectral overlap between the phosphorescence emission of CD@ Silica and the absorption curve of the dyes. Consequently, the FRET processes between b-CDs and dyes were triggered, resulting in a shift of the phosphorescence emission in the composite system from green to a longer wavelength. This enabled the realization of multi-color phosphorescence based on CD/fluorescence dyes@ Silica composites, as shown in Fig. 5 a.

The structure, wherein b-CDs and fluorescence dyes were embedded within the silica matrix through the covalent bonds, effectively protected the radiative transition from the triplet state, leading to multi-color phosphorescence emission with ultra-stable properties. As illustrated in Fig. S12, when CD@ Silica powders were immersed in an aqueous solution, hydrochloric acid solution, and sodium hydroxide solution for 24 h, phosphorescence signals from these samples became visible to the naked eye upon UV light irradiation. The phosphorescence images revealed no remarkable changes in the afterglow emission among composites. The afterglow intensity of these composites in various PH conditions was also measured, and the changes were shown in Fig. 5b. Only small fluctuation of phosphorescence intensity could be found in these multi-color composites at different PH changes. These composites also exhibit good stability under ultraviolet radiation. Three kinds of composite powders were irradiated under 365 nm for 5 days, and the afterglow intensity exhibited no obvious change (as shown in Fig. 5c). The thermal stability of the CD@ Silica composite was investigated by the thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) curves. As shown in Fig. S13, a minor weight loss for CD@ Silica when heated over 150 °C, which further demonstrated the starting dehydration processes in the CDs and silica shells. Stronger covalent bonds between CDs and silica enhance the afterglow intensity after the composite is annealed at elevated temperatures. These observations verified that the CD@ Silica and CD/fluorescence dyes@ Silica composites maintained ultra-stable phosphorescence emissions, even in complex environments. Therefore, the ultra-stable thermally enhanced multi-color RTP was realized in CD@ Silica composite system.

Due to their stable RTP emission properties, CD@ Silica phosphorescent materials have been successfully employed in fluorescence/afterglow multi-level information encryption. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) represents another typical matrix known to promote the radiative transitions from the triplet state in some CDs; however, the phosphorescence emission in CD@ PVA composites can be readily quenched by water. Meanwhile, Phos. ink-1 and Phos. ink-2 were formulated using b-CDs, CD@ Silica composites, and PVA. The encryption principle is illustrated in Fig. 6 a, wherein different data were written on a glass slide using Phos. ink-1, Phos. ink-2, and conventional fluorescence dyes. Under UV light irradiation, a blue “8888” pattern was displayed, while a green “2023” was captured by the naked eye when UV light was switched off, indicating the first level of the encrypted data (2023). After that, water was sprayed onto the glass slide, quenching the phosphorescence emission from the information written with Phos. Ink-2. Once the UV light was turned off, only the data “2021” remained visible, representing the second level of the encrypted data (2021). In addition, substances like sweat or oils secreted from fingers did not affect the ultra-stable phosphorescence emission. Hence, the CD@ Silica composite holds potential for applications in fingerprint recognition and anti-counterfeiting measures. Fig. 6b displays the images of fingerprints utilizing CD@ Silica composite powders as a labeling marker on glass. Under UV light illumination, distinct blue fluorescent emissive fingerprint patterns emerged. When the UV light was turned off, the green phosphorescent emissive fingerprints, including intricate details of finger ridge, were still clearly visible to the naked eye for up to 2 s, accomplishing the fingerprint phosphorescent encryption. Hence, the CD@ Silica composite not only eliminates the need for liquid labeling markers on the surface but also presents a new strategy for fingerprint anti-counterfeiting.

In summary, using a universal synthetic approach, we have successfully developed composite materials with ultra-stable RTP properties by embedding b-CDs within a silica matrix. The b-CDs were prepared via hydrothermal synthesis and encapsulated in silica through covalent bonds. This unique structure of as-prepared composites reduces non-radiative transitions. It enhances RTP properties and ensures it remains unaffected in complex environments, such as acid or alkaline aqueous solutions and UV light irradiation. Notably, the RTP properties of CD@ Silica composites can be enhanced by annealing them at a suitable temperature, offering a simple method to modulate the RTP emission. In addition, multi-color RTP emission was achieved by introducing fluorescence dyes into this composite system. The high energy transfer efficiency of the FRET process was manifested, resulting in a pronounced shift in the phosphorescence emission wavelength. Furthermore, building on the ultra-stable phosphorescence properties of the CD@ Silica composite, we devised strategies for fluorescence/afterglow multi-level information encryption and fingerprint anti-counterfeiting, demonstrating their great potential in the realm of information security.

Xin Bao: Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Zixuan Liu: Data curation. Zhen Tian: Formal analysis, Data curation. Wenquan Sun: Writing - original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Hui Wang: Software, Formal analysis. Xi Yuan: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Development of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (20210101161JC, YDZJ202301ZYTS254).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2023.120408.
